column2: — Granny, there just aren't any normal ones, — said Marta.
— Where have you ever seen normal ones?
Only let's not talk about grandfather, I know all about what a good man he was.
Let's leave that outside the brackets.
— Well, Martochka, say what you will, raising a child on your own is no easy thing.
Although I'm very glad that you dared to do such a thing at all.
— Who dared, now?
Just like that… It just turned out this way, — she said and looked off somewhere toward the cherry trees.
— And who do you have there?
— the grandmother asked insinuatingly.
— Oh, as if the girls haven't already told you.
— Well, they did, but I want to hear it from you.
— Two boys.
— Congratulations, little bird.
— There'll be cherries this year, — said Marta and rose to her feet.
Lilichka and Miyechka arrived after lunch, when the new door was already in place.
— How lovely!
— said Lilichka.
— They put it in who knows how, — retorted Marta.
— How is your father?
— asked the grandmother.
— Did he muddle everything up?
— Nothing new, — replied Lilichka, twirling the keys on her finger.
The new door was wooden, painted white.
Lilichka dragged over to the door a large clay pot in which grew some tree, the name of which no one knew except Maria.

After the grandmother had spent her whole childhood telling her what ficuses she had had as a little girl, and where they had been left behind, and that, you know, she ought to have kept them, Maria sat down, studied a catalog of all houseplants, and brought the grandmother five ficuses with different names and different leaves.
— Now at least you'll have ficuses enough, — Maria said then.
— Oh, but what do I need them for, — the grandmother would say.
— Everything's getting overgrown with grass as it is, and now we need a thicket indoors too.
Marta, the grandmother, Lilichka and Miyechka sat at the table outdoors.
Nearby a cherry tree had finished blooming, and you could see the little green beads hanging thickly on the branches.
Underfoot, amaranth spread together with the grass and the wild daisies.
The sun, which had already set its mind on going down, peeked in at them under the blue-and-white striped umbrella on its long leg, crooked like a crane's.
On the table lay yesterday's doughnuts and today's salad of radish, sour cream and egg.
— I've ordered some fish for you, — the grandmother said to Marta.
— Why for me?
— You need it.
Pregnant women must eat fish.
Marta raised an eyebrow.
— Girls, — the grandmother said to all three, — now that we've all gathered here, an exclusively female company…
— Well, not exclusively, — Miyechka interrupted.
Everyone looked at her, and Solomiya went on:
— Marta is now carrying two males around with her.
Marta again raised her raven-black thick eyebrow and looked off to one side.
— Well, we'll see about that yet, — said the grandmother.
— Maybe, God willing, the doctors made a mistake and it's two girls in there.
So here's what I'm telling you now, as the eldest among you all…
— Granny, — Lilichka interrupted again, — somehow you managed to hold out until ninety-six without doling out advice to us from the height of your years, and now look at this!
— Never lets me get a word out, — the grandmother nodded toward Lilichka.
— So then, girls, this may be the last time the four of us sit together like this…
— Oh, Gra-a-anny, — Miyechka drawled this time.
— You've been saying that since you were about seventy — and since then we've sat like this a hundred times…
— Yes, let grandmother speak, she's earned the right by now, — Marta cut in, holding the bowl of salad in her hands.
In the corner of her lips there was sour cream.
— Martochka, do you mean to say that I'm old?
— the grandmother asked with feigned indignation.
— Do what you want to do.
Don't listen to anyone.
Don't even listen to me, girls.

Just do things so that it's good for you yourselves, because afterward no one will look back at you and remember that you did something to make someone cozy, to make it bright for someone and free of drafts.
No one will remember.
And another thing, my dears, have children…
The grandmother glanced at Miyechka, and she answered at once:
— Let's first have Marta's boys, and then we can turn our attention to me!
— Granny, — Marta spoke up, — you started off in such a way that you could go straight onto a TED talk.
— Onto television?
— It's like television, only cooler.
But about children…
— Well, what about children, child?
Have children and know that without a husband it's hard to look after them.
Marta…
— Now there's a roundabout way to get to it!
— Lilichka laughed.
— I just knew this was all about Marta and the father of her two hypothetical boys.
— Grandmother, — said Marta, finishing the salad, — rest assured, I'm not pregnant by a black man.
— Who is he, Marta?
Is he a decent man?
— You want to ask whether he'll marry me?
— Oh, even I know by now that marriage these days is neither here nor there.
Right, Lilichka?
Lilichka laughed, threw back her head, raised her delicate arms in the air, and said:
— Who needs that marriage anyway?!
— He won't marry me, — said Marta, smacking her lips and scanning the table with her eyes for what else she might eat.
— As if anyone could manage to marry you in the first place, — said Miyechka.
— Even if he wanted to, it would hardly come to anything.
— Now that's not me, — Lilichka snorted.
— Said 'I want to marry you,' and off I went right away, and only came to my senses some seven years later.
Miyechka, for her part, fell to thinking, recalled Danylo, his white fingers and long nails, his snow-white feet and his underwear folded away in drawers.
She imagined taking his hand and saying: 'Yes, I agree,' — and someone nearby is crying, and she is crying, but these tears are not from joy.

More than once she had dreamed that she was getting ready for a wedding and something kept getting in the way — now she can't find her shoes, now the dress has torn, now her period has started, now someone is dying and she is suddenly at the wheel of a hearse.
Then she remembered Ustym, whom she had met by chance at a dinner at friends', and how she had sat the whole evening looking at him, until a girlfriend told her to pull herself together.
Ustym had different hands, a different smell, and he had no underwear perfectly folded away in drawers.
'It's other women, other women who behave like this, others, not me,' she thought to herself.
That evening a stork flew down onto a post in the yard and sat there a long time, until late in the evening.
Everyone watched in wonder as the bird stood out, a black silhouette against the sky, and everyone wished it would spend the night with them.
They got up quietly from under the umbrella and walked slowly so as not to frighten the stork.
And besides, the grandmother could not walk fast.
But even Lilichka wasn't stomping like an elephant; she walked on tiptoe, swaying on her springy, slender legs.
Miyechka had found somewhere a striped cotton-print dress and in it looked like the housewives of the sixties.
She led the grandmother by the arm, and Marta carried in her hands the plates and the tablecloth, so the cats wouldn't drag them off the table in the night.

The stork just sat there: it did not stir from its spot while this column of four women walked toward the house along the path between the knotgrass, the grass and the daisies, lightly waving off the mosquitoes, theatrically, as if they were putting on a play for children.
The stork did not fly off even late in the evening.
Now it was barely visible.
The sky grew darker with every minute.

They all put on their nightgowns: Lilichka hers — with the buttons cut off, Miyechka hers — with the buttons left on and lace below the knees, Marta — a red silk one from Victoria's Secret, and the grandmother — a sky-blue one with a tiny fluffy sheep over the heart.
Outside it was quiet: not a single twig swayed, not a single blade of grass stirred.
You could hear the old cat snuffling in the kitchen.
— It's late for the stork to be building a nest, — said the grandmother, looking out the window into the gray night.
— As it is for me.
Too late, — said Marta.

— You're still quite little, — the grandmother wanted to hug Marta the way she had when Marta was small, the way she once was, when she would angrily bury her sturdy head with its coarse black hair into the grandmother's belly and cry, muffled, into the grandmother's bosom.
Instead, now, rather than drawing Marta to herself, she nestled against her, as if she herself were a little girl.
But Marta felt the way she had thirty-five years ago, when she was not forty but five.

Holding the grandmother by the shoulders, the way the grandmother had once held her, she nonetheless felt that she herself had become small, and, despite the fact that now it was the grandmother burying her head into her bosom, it seemed to Marta that it was all the other way around — that she was the little girl and her hair was still soft, and the grandmother was big and strong.
Pumpkins
Only three days had passed since the red Honda had brought Lilichka and Miyechka into the yard in their forty-year-old shoes.
And only two days since that same unchanging Honda had brought Marta and her future children.
They had replaced the door, washed the house, and walked, the three of them, through the raspberry thickets, plucking off the berries.
The grandmother stood on the porch that led out from the bedroom where Lilichka and Miyechka slept, warming their cold feet against each other.
— This raspberry has dwindled away, — said the grandmother.
— And once your grandfather brought it from Tashkent.
— Since when was Tashkent famous for raspberries?
— asked Miyechka.
— This raspberry, — the grandmother went on, — used to be like a good thumb.
This big!
— she held up her thumb.
— And then, you see, it dwindled and became like thimbles.
Lilichka and Miyechka were barely visible in the raspberry, but Marta had been tall since childhood.
The tallest of her peers.
Her proud profile was visible always and everywhere.
And now too, in the raspberry.
— Martochka, and where exactly are you going to give birth?
— asked the grandmother, leaning on the railing.
— Anywhere at all, just not here, — retorted Marta.
— Well, how about that, — said the grandmother.
— To live your whole life here, building a bright future, only for your granddaughters to be afraid to give birth here.
And while the dwindled raspberry was being eaten away by the birds and the three sisters, Lilichka, one morning when no one had yet even brewed coffee, steeped tea, or fried eggs, said:
— Let's sow some pumpkins.
— It's too late now to plant pumpkins, — replied the grandmother.
— Everything's too late for you, — retorted Marta.
— Too late for storks to build a nest, too late to plant pumpkins.
Then it's too late for me to give birth too.
Lilichka glanced at the grandmother, put her fingers to her lips, as if to say there's no point irritating Marta, and said:
— Pumpkins for photos.
And then we'll sell them for Halloween.
— For photos?
— asked the grandmother.
— Can't you just go and photograph some pumpkins somewhere?
Marta was sucking the honey off her fingers — in the mornings she would go straight to the pantry and dig at the honey from two years back with a big wooden spoon.
— Lilichka is right, — she spoke up.
— We can't just sit around doing nothing for three months.
— If you don't want to sit around doing nothing, then teach the local children languages, — said the grandmother.
— Miyechka, what did you learn French for?
To order 'le croissant' once a year?
— So what about the pumpkins?
— asked Miyechka, avoiding even thinking about children.
— Well, look, — said Lilichka.
— We take pumpkins, plant them now, by autumn they ripen, and at Halloween we sell them.
There are buyers.
Near Marseille.
On the condition that each pumpkin has a portfolio.
— To haul pumpkins across all of Europe?
— asked the grandmother.
— Granny, everything's different now.
Nowadays they haul things from China.
And these will be organic pumpkins — we'll photograph each one as it grows, each — from the little seed, from the sprout, from the shoot, from the flower and… — a finished pumpkin.
— For pumpkins like that, — said Marta, — you can get fifty apiece.
And that's just what we'll get.
A wonderful idea.
Lilichka stood with a notebook, her hair still uncombed from the night, little chestnut strands falling onto her fair forehead.
— Miyechka, — the grandmother spoke up, — and you really think they'll sell pumpkins for such silly money?
— Well, even if they don't sell them, — said Miyechka.
— The land lies empty and grows over with who knows what, and this way at least there'll be pumpkins.
Beautiful ones.
— Well, someone will take them later, — the grandmother agreed.
— Great-grandmother used to say — don't let the land lie idle, — retorted Lilichka, suddenly feeling a calling to farming.
Together they carried a little sack of seeds outside.
There were to be only the classic, round, pot-bellied pumpkins.
— Any others I never recognized, — said the grandmother.
They brought her out a little folding stool.
The blackcurrants were beginning to ripen, and Marta went about eating the barely blackened berries.
Every now and then the grandmother tried to find out about Marta's future, but Marta planned no further than three months ahead and planted pumpkins together with her sisters.
But the grandmother, just as delicately as she had tried to find out about the father of Marta's twins, kept hinting that it was too late to plant pumpkins.
That they wouldn't come up.
And if they came up, they'd wither, and if they didn't wither, they wouldn't ripen in time for Halloween.
— Marta, do you want me to sign you up for an antenatal course?
— Lilichka would say.
— I'll drive you there like a loving man, and treat you to decaf coffee afterward.
— What do I need those courses for?
— Marta would say.
